claim: Many proteins in human cells can be post-translationally modified at lysine residues via acetylation.

result.claim: Many proteins in human cells can be post-translationally modified at lysine residues via acetylation.
result.reasoning: The claim is strongly supported by the evidence. Paper ID 2831620, in proposition 1527 and chunk 48, explicitly states that "Lysine acetylation is a reversible posttranslational modification" and further elaborates that it involves "transfer of an acetyl group from acetyl CoA to lysine e- amino group of targeted protein". This directly confirms that proteins can be post-translationally modified at lysine residues via acetylation. While the paper does not explicitly mention "human cells," the description of lysine acetylation as a fundamental biological process implies its presence across eukaryotes, including humans.

evidence.text: The assay was applied by expressing mutated proteins as transgenes in cell lines.
evidence.source: Identification and Functional Characterization of N-Terminally Acetylated Proteins in Drosophila melanogaster

evidence.text: Increasing microtubule acetylation using deacetylase inhibitors or the tubulin acetylase αTAT1 prevents association of mutant LRRK2 with microtubules.
evidence.source: Increasing microtubule acetylation rescues axonal transport and locomotor deficits caused by LRRK2 Roc-COR domain mutations

evidence.text: The study profiled global mRNA and chromatin changes in committed, transit-amplifying progeny.
evidence.source: Genome-wide maps of histone modifications unwind in vivo chromatin states of the hair follicle lineage.

evidence.text: CH6-LNPs-siRNA enhanced mechanical properties in rodents.
evidence.source: Aptamer-functionalized lipid nanoparticles targeting osteoblasts as a novel RNA interference–based bone anabolic strategy

evidence.text: Ras-induced macropinocytosis of extracellular proteins reduces a transformed cell's dependence on extracellular glutamine.

evidence.text: Amino-terminal acetylation of proteins is a common modification found throughout the tree of life.

evidence.text: Lysine acetylation involves in cell immune status and degenerative diseases like Alzheimer’s disease and Huntington’s disease.
evidence.source: Protein Lysine Acetylated/Deacetylated Enzymes and the Metabolism-Related Diseases

evidence.text: Disorders of lysine acetylation may be correlated with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.

evidence.text: Lysine acetylation involves the transfer of an acetyl group from acetyl CoA to a lysine e- amino group of a targeted protein.

evidence.text: Lysine acetylation is modulated by acetyltransferases and deacetylases.

evidence.text: Lysine acetylation is a reversible posttranslational modification.

evidence.text: The authors enzymatically depleted HA by a clinically formulated PEGylated human recombinant PH20 hyaluronidase (PEGPH20).

evidence.text: Introduction of the methionine and an adjacent alanine residue into a range of noncognate CheYs, dramatically changed their specificity, allowing protein interaction and rapid phosphotransfer from CheA(3)-P.

evidence.text: N-terminal acetylation occurs in insects with a similar frequency as in humans.

evidence.text: The function of N-terminal acetylation can be studied in all species.
evidence.source: Identification and Functional Characterization of N-Terminally Acetylated Proteins in Drosophila melanogaster

evidence.text: Lysosomal degradation of extracellular proteins can sustain cell survival and induce activation of mTORC1.
evidence.source: The Utilization of Extracellular Proteins as Nutrients Is Suppressed by mTORC1

evidence.text: Site-directed mutagenesis of this methionine in combination with two adjacent residues abolished binding, as shown by surface plasmon resonance studies, and phosphotransfer from CheA(3)-P to CheY(6).
evidence.source: Using Structural Information to Change the Phosphotransfer Specificity of a Two-Component Chemotaxis Signalling Complex
